title: Lesson 6: Worship and the Glory of God
description: Worship is gladly reflecting back to God the radiance of His worth. This lesson explores what it means to worship God in spirit and truth - not just in a Sunday service but as the posture of your whole life.

blocks.text: You have walked through the heart of the Trinity - loving the Father, finding your identity in the Son, and learning to walk in the power of the Spirit. Now a question emerges from all of that: what is the natural response? When you genuinely encounter who God is, what happens? The answer is the topic of this lesson. The deepest, most natural response to knowing God is worship. And worship, as you are about to discover, is far bigger and more alive than anything that happens on a Sunday morning.
What Worship Actually Is

Most people think of worship as a style of music, or a portion of a church service where someone plays guitar. But that is like saying eating is what happens in a restaurant. It might happen there - but eating is so much larger than that.

John Piper defines worship this way: Worship is the act of gladly reflecting back to God the radiance of His worth. Unpack that slowly. Gladly - it is not reluctant obligation. Reflecting back - you are displaying something that originates in Him, not in you. The radiance of His worth - not your performance, not your feelings, but His infinite value. Worship, at its core, is seeing God as He is and letting that sight shape everything you do.
Jesus gave the clearest definition of what God is looking for from His worshippers:

But the hour is coming, and is now here, when the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth, for the Father is seeking such people to worship him. God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.

Two words anchor this: spirit and truth. Spirit means it is genuine - coming from the inside, not a performance. Truth means it is anchored in who God actually is - not a God we invented to make us feel good, but the God of Scripture, in all His holiness and love. Worship that is only emotional without truth becomes manipulation. Worship that is only doctrinal without heart becomes a lecture. God wants both.
5.1 - Before this lesson, what would you have said worship means? How does Piper's phrase - 'gladly reflecting back to God the radiance of His worth' - expand or challenge that?
The God Who Seeks Worshippers

Notice something striking in John 4:23: "The Father is seeking such people to worship him." God is not passive. He is not waiting to see if we will bother to worship. He is actively seeking true worshippers. Let that land. The God of the universe is on a search - and what He is looking for is people who will see Him clearly and respond to Him honestly.

Isaiah caught a glimpse of this once. In a vision, he saw the Lord in the temple - high and lifted up, with the train of His robe filling the temple. Seraphim were calling out, "Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!" The doorposts shook. Isaiah fell apart: "Woe is me! For I am lost; for I am a man of unclean lips." Then a coal from the altar touched his lips and he was forgiven. Then he heard the voice of God: "Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?" And Isaiah responded: "Here I am! Send me."

That is the arc of true worship. See God clearly, feel your own smallness, receive grace, respond in surrender. It moves from vision to humility to cleansing to commission. Worship is not a feeling you generate. It is an encounter that changes what you do next.
5.2 - When in your life have you had a genuine moment of encounter with who God is - a moment that produced not just feeling but a real response in how you lived? What happened?
Worship Is a Posture, Not an Event
Romans 12 is where Paul makes his most sweeping statement about what a worshipping life actually looks like:
I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship.

Paul does not say worship is something you do on Sunday. He says worship is what happens when you present your body as a living sacrifice. Your commute. Your workday. Your kitchen table. Your conversations with coworkers. Your parenting. The way you handle money, anger, time, and relationships. All of it - it is all your worship. The old sacrifices in Israel were whole animals placed on an altar and consumed by fire. Paul is saying: that is what your whole life is supposed to be. Not a donation to God's hour on Sunday. A complete surrender for every hour of every day.

Worship is not a genre of music. It is a posture of the heart. It is what happens when you make God's worth the measuring stick for every decision you make. When you ask "Is what I'm about to do reflecting God's worth back to the world, or am I reaching for a counterfeit?" - that is worship thinking. It is radically practical.
5.3 - What are you practically magnifying with your time, treasure, and attention right now? Looking at your average week, what does your life say you value most?
Worship When It Hurts

There is a moment in the book of Acts that most people hurry past, but it stops you cold when you slow down. Paul and Silas have been beaten, bloody, and thrown into prison for preaching the gospel. Their feet are in stocks. It is the middle of the night. And Acts 16:25 says:
About midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them.

The prisoners were listening. Of course they were. They had never seen anything like it. Beaten men singing. People in chains praising. In the worst moment of the night, these two men were worshipping. Not because their circumstances were good. Because their God was good. Worship that only happens when things go well is not really declaring God's worth - it is declaring our comfort. But worship that rises from a dungeon at midnight? That is saying something that the whole world is listening for.

The Psalms give us a vocabulary for this. Nearly half of the Psalms are laments - honest, raw cries of pain and confusion to God. Worship does not require you to pretend you are fine. But it does require you to being honest with God about where you are, and then turning your gaze to who He is even in the middle of it.
Why are you cast down, O my soul, and why are you in turmoil within me? Hope in God; for I shall again praise him, my salvation and my God.

5.4 - Is there a hard season in your life right now - or one you have walked through recently - where worship was or would have been difficult? What would it take for you to worship God genuinely in that moment, not because things are good, but because He is?
Corporate Worship - Why You Need the Body

Personal worship is real. But God designed worship to be practiced with His people gathered together, not only alone. Psalm 150 tells a vision of corporate praise - all the instruments, all the voices, everything that has breath praising the Lord. And Hebrews 10 gives us the practical reason to keep gathering:

And let us consider how to stir up one another to love and good works, not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near.

You cannot worship in full isolation. Part of the genius of gathered worship is that when your faith runs low, other people's faith sustains you. Their voices carry the song when you cannot find the words. When you see a room full of people who have tasted and seen that the Lord is good, declaring His praise, it reinforces what you know in your own heart but may be struggling to feel. You need the body. The body needs you.

5.5 - How has being part of a worshipping community - a church - strengthened or changed your personal walk with God? What has the gathered church given you that you could not have gotten on your own?
This Week's Practice

Try a simple experiment this week. Pick one mundane task you do every day - making coffee, commuting, cooking dinner - and during that activity, practice directing your attention toward God. You can speak to Him, listen to a Psalm being read aloud, or simply express one honest thing about who He is. Not a performance. Not an obligation. Just a turning-toward.

Also this week, read Psalm 95 and Psalm 96 back to back. Read them out loud if you can. Notice what kind of language they use - the specific words for praising God's character. Then read Psalm 42 and let the honesty of it land. You are learning the full vocabulary of worship: triumphant and lamenting, corporate and personal, feeling it and choosing it.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
